Documentation

🚀Getting Started📦Installation🔄Transactions & Async✏️CRUD Operations🔍Querying🤖Source Generators📡Change Data Capture🌍Spatial Indexing🏗️Architecture🆔Custom ID Converters⚡Benchmarks🔌Schema-less API🔎BLQL📈TimeSeries🗝️Key-Value Store

🔍 Querying

Master BLite's powerful LINQ-based query engine with automatic index optimization.

Basic Queries

var results = users.AsQueryable()
    .Where(u => u.Age > 25)
    .AsEnumerable();

foreach (var user in results)
{
    Console.WriteLine(user.Name);
}

Filtering

Comparison Operators

// Equality
.Where(u => u.Name == "Alice")

// Greater than / Less than
.Where(u => u.Age > 30)
.Where(u => u.Age <= 50)

// Range
.Where(u => u.Age >= 18 && u.Age <= 65)

String Operations

// StartsWith (optimized with B-Tree index)
.Where(u => u.Name.StartsWith("A"))

// Contains (full scan)
.Where(u => u.Email.Contains("@example.com"))

// Case-insensitive
.Where(u => u.Name.ToLower() == "alice")

Logical Operators

// AND
.Where(u => u.Age > 25 && u.Name.StartsWith("A"))

// OR
.Where(u => u.Age < 18 || u.Age > 65)

// NOT
.Where(u => !(u.Age >= 18 && u.Age <= 65))

Sorting

// Ascending
.OrderBy(u => u.Age)

// Descending
.OrderByDescending(u => u.Name)

// Multiple sort keys
.OrderBy(u => u.Age)
.ThenBy(u => u.Name)

Pagination

int page = 2;
int pageSize = 10;

var results = users.AsQueryable()
    .OrderBy(u => u.Id)
    .Skip((page - 1) * pageSize)
    .Take(pageSize)
    .AsEnumerable();

Projection

Select Specific Fields

var names = users.AsQueryable()
    .Select(u => u.Name)
    .AsEnumerable();

// Anonymous types
var summary = users.AsQueryable()
    .Select(u => new { u.Name, u.Age })
    .AsEnumerable();

Push-down Projection NEW in v1.5.0

When a query contains only scalar-field projections, BLite compiles the SELECT lambda into a single-pass raw-BSON reader. The source type T is never instantiated — only the requested bytes are read off the page.

// Only Name and Age bytes are read — User is never allocated
var dtos = users.AsQueryable()
    .Select(u => new UserDto(u.Name, u.Age))
    .ToList();

// WHERE + SELECT: both evaluated in a single BSON pass
var adults = users.AsQueryable()
    .Where(u => u.Age >= 18)
    .Select(u => new { u.Name, u.Age })
    .ToList();
⚡ When push-down fires: SELECT with scalar fields only, with or without a WHERE clause. Queries that include OrderBy / Take / Skip after the projection still benefit — the ordering/paging is applied on the already-projected IEnumerable<TResult>. Complex/nested fields (e.g. x.Address) automatically fall back to the standard path.

Async LINQ v1.4.0+

AsQueryable() returns IBLiteQueryable<T>, which preserves the async chain across all LINQ operators.

// Async terminal operators
var list  = await users.AsQueryable().Where(u => u.Age > 25).ToListAsync();
var count = await users.AsQueryable().CountAsync();
var any   = await users.AsQueryable().AnyAsync(u => u.Active);

// Async streaming via IAsyncEnumerable<T>
await foreach (var user in users.AsQueryable().Where(u => u.Age > 25).AsAsyncEnumerable())
{
    Console.WriteLine(user.Name);
}

Aggregations

// Count
var total = users.AsQueryable().Count();
var adults = users.AsQueryable().Count(u => u.Age >= 18);

// Sum, Min, Max, Average
var totalAge = users.AsQueryable().Sum(u => u.Age);
var youngest = users.AsQueryable().Min(u => u.Age);
var oldest = users.AsQueryable().Max(u => u.Age);
var avgAge = users.AsQueryable().Average(u => u.Age);

GroupBy

var byAge = users.AsQueryable()
    .GroupBy(u => u.Age)
    .Select(g => new
    {
        Age = g.Key,
        Count = g.Count(),
        Names = g.Select(u => u.Name).ToList()
    })
    .AsEnumerable();

Index Optimization

BLite automatically uses B-Tree indexes when available:

// Create an index on Age
users.EnsureIndex(u => u.Age);

// This query now uses the index
var results = users.AsQueryable()
    .Where(u => u.Age > 30) // ← Index hit!
    .AsEnumerable();

// Enum properties can also be indexed (v1.7.0)
orders.EnsureIndex(o => o.Status);
var shipped = orders.AsQueryable()
    .Where(o => o.Status == OrderStatus.Shipped)
    .AsEnumerable();
💡 Tip: Use .Explain() to see query execution plans and verify index usage. Enum index keys are stored as their underlying integer value (int, byte, long) — ordering and range queries work correctly.

Nested Property Indexes NEW in v3.0.0

Index on properties inside embedded sub-objects using a standard lambda path. BLite derives the B-Tree key path automatically and skips records where an intermediate property is null.

// Configure in OnModelCreating
modelBuilder.Entity<Customer>()
    .HasIndex(x => x.Address.City);      // → path "address.city"

modelBuilder.Entity<Order>()
    .HasIndex(x => x.Shipping.Address.PostalCode);  // deeper nesting OK

// The LINQ engine picks up the index automatically
var customers = db.Customers.AsQueryable()
    .Where(c => c.Address.City == "Milan")
    .ToList();  // ← B-Tree hit on "address.city"

var orders = db.Orders.AsQueryable()
    .Where(o => o.Shipping.Address.PostalCode == "20100")
    .ToList();
⚙️ How it works: BLite walks the lambda path at index-time to extract the leaf value. If any intermediate property is null, the document is silently skipped — no exception is thrown and the document is simply not indexed for that field.

Advanced Examples

Complex Filter

var results = users.AsQueryable()
    .Where(u => 
        u.Age >= 25 && 
        u.Age <= 50 && 
        u.Name.StartsWith("A") &&
        u.Email.Contains("@company.com")
    )
    .OrderBy(u => u.Age)
    .ThenBy(u => u.Name)
    .Take(100)
    .AsEnumerable();
